page: UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-16xCPU-128GB is a memory-optimized virtual server hosted on the KVM hypervisor. It features 16 shared vCPUs mapped to 16 physical cores of an AMD EPYC 7542 processor running at 2.0 GHz. The system is equipped with 128.0 GB of RAM, providing a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU. This configuration does not include local storage or bundled GPUs, relying instead on network-attached storage and external network configurations, with zero complimentary public IPv4 addresses included.

Performance benchmarks position this server in the average tier for multi-core operations, though single-core performance is relatively weak. The stress-ng single-core CPU test ranks in the bottom 10%, and single-threaded gzip compression falls in the bottom 25%. However, multi-core execution is robust, with stress-ng multi-core, Geekbench multi-core, and PassMark CPU scores all landing in the middle 50%. Memory bandwidth is stable, averaging 107.48 GB/sec for uncached large-block reads and 76.69 GB/sec for uncached large-block writes.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, the shared CPU allocation model offers a resource-dense memory profile without the premium cost of dedicated compute cores. This makes the server highly suitable for memory-intensive workloads that do not require sustained, peak single-core CPU performance. Appropriate workloads include database operations, Redis caching layers, software compilation, and static web serving, where the 128.0 GB memory capacity can be fully leveraged.
description: UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-16xCPU-128GB is a memory-optimized virtual server utilizing the KVM hypervisor. It is configured with 16 shared vCPUs powered by an AMD EPYC 7542 processor running at 2.0 GHz, paired with 128.0 GB of system memory. This architecture provides a high memory-to-core ratio of 8.0 GB per vCPU, though it lacks local storage and bundled GPUs. Benchmark results indicate average multi-core performance and memory bandwidth, contrasted by weaker single-core capabilities, with stress-ng single-core performance ranking in the bottom 10%. The shared CPU model offers a cost-effective solution for memory-heavy applications. This server is well-suited for database operations, Redis caching, and static web serving.
